text: In this video we present the case of sixty nine years old man, who developed a left ureteral stricture in presence of a nine millimeters stone.
The ureteroscopy reveals a tight ureteral stricture in the left ureter, visible as a narrowed segment within the ureteral lumen.
A nephrostomy tube is visible, and a combined pyelography reveals a ureteral stricture approximately three centimeters in length.
A robot assisted ureteroplasty with buccal mucosa graft was planned.
Key points of our procedure are reported in this slide.
The surgeon performs a flexible ureteroscopy, inserting a guide wire through the visible ureteral stricture.
The surgeon prepares the ureter using translumination and the Firefly tool, enhancing visualization of the ureteral structure.
The surgeon characterizes the ureteral stricture using three techniques, assessing its length, boundaries, and tissue quality.
The surgeon uses translumination with a ureteroscope and the firefly tool to visually determine the lower limit of the ureteral stricture.
The surgeon determines the upper limit of the ureteral stricture using transnephrostomic injection of indocyanine green (ICG), which highlights the stricture under imaging.
Intravenous ICG injection is administered, providing a visual assessment of the tissue quality within the ureteral stricture area.
The surgeon incises the ureteral wall while preserving the posterior plate, and subsequently removes a visible stone from the ureter.
The surgeon harvests a graft of buccal mucosa from the cheek and sutures it with four half sutures to prevent curling, ensuring the graft remains flat.
The surgeon places a ureteral stent using an endoscope, guiding it into position within the ureter.
The surgeon opens Girota's fascia, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The ureteroscope provides transillumination, clearly illuminating the left ureter to facilitate its identification and guide the dissection process.

The surgeon performs a transnephrostomic ICG injection, followed by endoscopic transillumination, to visually identify the proximal and distal ends of the ureteral stricture, aiding in precise dissection.
The surgeon incises the proximal end of the ureteral stricture, revealing the guide wire within the lumen.
The surgeon incises the distal end of the stricture and makes a longitudinal incision along the anterior wall of the stricture.
The surgeon preserves the posterior plate during the procedure, maintaining its integrity while working around the surgical site.
The surgeon progressively removes a stone that is impacted in the ureteral mucosa, carefully extracting it from the ureter.
The surgeon measures the length of the ureteral stricture using a caliper, ensuring precise assessment of the affected segment.
The surgeon performs a complete flexible ureteroscopy, visually inspecting the ureter for any residual stones.

The surgeon sutures the buccal mucosa graft, harvested from the patient's cheek, onto the surgical site using a 4-0 absorbable suture in a running fashion, with four half sutures to prevent curling of the patch.
The surgeon creates an omentum flap, manipulating the omental tissue to form a flap for surgical use.
The surgeon applies Teseal to protect the buccal mucosa graft patch and the surrounding area.
The surgeon wraps the patch in the omentum flap to enhance its vascularization.
Operative time was two hundred thirty minutes, console time one hundred fifty minutes, no perioperative complications were reported.
The bladder catheter was removed after four days, the abdominal drain was removed after five days, nephrostomy tube was closed after ten days, ureteral stent was removed after thirty days.
Nephrostomy was eventually removed after thirty two days.
A CT scan was performed before the complete removal of nephrostomy.
two month after surgery the patient is asymptomatic without hydronephrosis
